serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kerabu Pucuk Paku is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small side-dish portion served alongside white rice in a Malay meal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — HPB Foodo; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (fiddlehead ferns, coconut, dried shrimp components)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for Kerabu Pucuk Paku exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for traditional Malay vegetable salads and USDA FoodData Central entries for fiddlehead ferns (USDA FDC ID 169247), toasted coconut, and dried shrimp. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with these component-level references and are retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g based on kerisik (toasted coconut) contribution; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in component sources for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetables and high-fibre salad composites
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kerabu Pucuk Paku. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous non-starchy vegetable salad composites from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is dominated by fiddlehead ferns (low-GI non-starchy vegetable), kerisik (fat-rich toasted coconut slowing gastric emptying), and lime juice dressing. High dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g) and fat content further attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (25 × 8.2) / 100 = 2.1 g, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg per 100 g from dried shrimp and belacan
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced dried shrimp and belacan in the dressing, as these ingredients contribute approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g; the dish's high potassium content (320 mg/100 g) partially offsets sodium load and supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and high dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g) with low net carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kerabu Pucuk Paku is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low GI (~25), high fibre content (3.5 g/100 g), and modest net carbohydrate (approximately 4.7 g/100 g after fibre) result in a very low glycemic load of 2.1 per serving, supporting postprandial blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from kerisik (toasted coconut) estimated at ~4.5 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish provides beneficial dietary fibre, potassium, and antioxidant vitamins (A, C, E), the saturated fat contribution from kerisik (estimated ~4.5 g/100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; sodium from dried shrimp and belacan should also be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kerabu Pucuk Paku is a traditional Malay wild fern salad originating from Malaysia and widely consumed across the Malay Peninsula. The dish centres on 'pucuk paku' — the young fronds of the edible fiddlehead fern (Diplazium esculentum) — which are briefly blanched to preserve their vibrant green colour, delicate earthy flavour, and characteristic slight crunch. The blanched ferns are tossed with 'kerisik', a preparation of freshly grated coconut that has been dry-toasted and pounded until it releases its natural oils, imparting a rich, nutty, and slightly caramelised depth to the salad. The dressing is built from lime juice for acidity, dried shrimp (udang kering) for umami and salinity, thinly sliced shallots, and bird's eye chilies for heat. Shrimp paste (belacan) is often incorporated in small amounts for fermented depth. Fresh aromatics such as lemongrass or ginger torch flower (bunga kantan) may be added for floral and citrus notes. Nutritionally, Kerabu Pucuk Paku provides approximately 115 kcal per 100 g, with 3.8 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly from coconut), and 8.2 g carbohydrate. It is notably high in dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g), vitamin A (450 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin K (120 mcg/100 g), folate (85 mcg/100 g), and potassium (320 mg/100 g). Its low glycemic index (~25) and very low glycemic load (2.1 per 100 g serving) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content (280 mg/100 g) from dried shrimp and belacan requires monitoring for hypertension. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is served as a side dish alongside white rice in Malay home cooking and festive meals.
common_user_queries: Is fiddlehead fern salad good for diabetics?
Is Kerabu Pucuk Paku low GI?
Can hypertension patients eat Kerabu Pucuk Paku?
What does Kerabu Pucuk Paku taste like?
meal_context: Healthy vegetable side with white rice

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for Kerabu Pucuk Paku exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for traditional Malay vegetable salads and USDA FoodData Central component-level entries for fiddlehead ferns, toasted coconut, and dried shrimp. The AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these references and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable composites. Saturated fat is estimated from kerisik contribution. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct published composite analysis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for this traditional Malay fern salad preparation are not available in HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this dish is plant-based with dried shrimp as the only animal-derived ingredient; vitamin B12 content is negligible and reported as confirmed zero based on the composition of the primary ingredients per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: fiddlehead ferns and coconut do not contribute meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 4. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g per 100 g based on kerisik (toasted coconut) contribution, consistent with USDA FoodData Central coconut data. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable salad composites; no direct GI measurement for Kerabu Pucuk Paku has been published. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (25 × 8.2) / 100 = 2.1 g (very low). 7. Sodium of 280 mg/100 g reflects typical dried shrimp and belacan usage; actual sodium will vary with recipe.
